games.explanation: The market is treating this like a coin flip because Cleveland's uniform still carries weight, but Miami is quietly the better team on paper and getting a plus-adjacent price to prove it. Tyler Phillips isn't missing bats, but he's kept the barrels down and the walks manageable against a Cleveland lineup that's been one of the league's least threatening all year. The Marlins counter with a top of the order (Lopez, Hernandez, Edwards) that grades out well above what Cleveland will run out there, and the projected margin is closer to a full run than the price suggests. At -110 on the better roster, you're basically getting the coin flip for free.

Ginn's overall line looks respectable until you notice it's built almost entirely on shutting down righties, and the White Sox are running a left-handed heavy top of the order right into him. Against lefties he's coughing up a .351 xwOBA with barrels flying off the bat, which is exactly what Benintendi, Vargas and Teel are shaped to exploit. Chicago is also the meaningfully better team here by Elo, so getting them at a small favorite price feels like the market still can't stomach putting the Sox on the correct side of a number. Kurtz will do Kurtz things, but one bat doesn't cover a starter who's a coin flip once the platoon flips against him. -125 is the tax for admitting the obvious.

Emmet Sheehan is a strikeout arm with a walk problem, and the market is treating him like he's the finished product instead of the work in progress. Against lefties he's been walking hitters at a double-digit clip and giving up barrels like it's batting practice, which is a rough scouting report when Carroll and Marte are the first two names in the box. Arizona's projected score sits within a tenth of a run of Los Angeles despite the Elo gap, meaning the sim sees a coin flip that the board has priced as a mismatch. Getting +190 on a lineup this deep against a rookie who hasn't solved his command is the entire ballgame.

Toronto is priced like the safer bet, but a coin flip with plus money on the road is the kind of thing the board tends to shave when the favorite has a recognizable name on the mound. Gausman is a fine pitcher who has quietly been ordinary against righties this year, and San Diego's top four all swing from that side with Tatis and Machado doing real damage to hard stuff. Marquez is the obvious hole in the boat, but the projected score sits at 4.6 to 4.5, meaning the sim already knows he's bad and still likes the Padres. Getting +110 on the better lineup in a game the market has as a near pick-em is the whole argument.

Arizona's projected score sits within a tenth of a run of Los Angeles despite the Elo gap, meaning the sim sees a coin flip that the board has priced as a mismatch. Getting +190 on a lineup this deep against a rookie who hasn't solved his command is the entire ballgame.

The market is pricing this like a mismatch when the projected score has it landing inside a single run, which is exactly the gap the run line was invented to exploit. Emmet Sheehan misses bats but pays for it with walks, and the top of the Arizona order is built to punish that profile: Marte, Carroll and Perdomo all run xwOBAs north of .350 against righties. Chase Field's homer factor doesn't hurt when you've got three barrel-hunters loaded at the top of the card, and Sheehan's hard-hit rate against lefties sits at a queasy 50 percent. Dodgers can win this game and still not cover, which is the whole point of taking a plus-price six outs of insurance.

Toronto is priced like the safer bet, but a coin flip with plus money on the road is the kind of thing the board tends to shave when the favorite has a recognizable name on the mound. Gausman is a fine pitcher who has quietly been ordinary against righties this year, and San Diego's top four all swing from that side with Tatis and Machado doing real damage to hard stuff. Marquez is the obvious hole in the boat, but the projected score sits at 4.6 to 4.5, meaning the sim already knows he's bad and still likes the Padres. Getting +110 on the better lineup in a game the market has as a near pick-em is the whole argument.
